title: Divergent Social Worlds: Neighborhood Crime and the Racial-Spatial Divide By Ruth D. Peterson and Lauren J. Krivo Russell Sage Foundation. 2010. 184 pages. $37.50 cloth, $24.95 paper
abstract: Among the most central questions in the study of neighborhood outcomes concerns the dramatically higher rates of crime, and especially violence, in minority versus White areas. What accounts for the unequal patterning of crime by race and ethnicity, where the violent crime rate in many minority neighborhoods is 2-5 times higher than in white ones? The predominant explanation, rooted in seminal work by Shaw and McKay (1942), links variation in crime rates to the structural and social organization of neighborhoods rather than the composition of individual residents. This prevailing “racial invariance hypothesis” contends that the stark divide in the structural realities of White and minority neighborhoods accounts for the drastic differences in crime rates by race and ethnicity. If, in ­contrast to the current urban landscape, non-White neighborhoods were as advantaged as white areas, we would expect very similar rates of violence and property crime. Ruth D. Peterson and Lauren J. Krivo's important book is the culmination of an impressive data collection effort and stands as the most comprehensive and persuasive test of the racial invariance hypothesis to date. Like others, Peterson and Krivo attempt to explain severe differences in violent crime, and to a lesser extent property crime, by pointing to the divergent social worlds of minority versus white neighborhoods. Their book makes substantial theoretical and empirical contributions beyond previous research. In terms of theory, Peterson and Krivo broaden our understanding of racial urban inequality by focusing not only on the internal characteristics of neighborhoods, but also the unequal ways neighborhoods are embedded spatially by race and ethnicity. Viewing ethno-racial urban inequality in the United States as a “racial-spatial divide” requires a more holistic theoretical lens than traditional criminological explanations. Referencing recent theory by Bonilla-Silva (2001) and others, Peterson and Krivo advocate for understanding race as a principal organizing structure in society, and point to racial segregation as the key mechanism undergirding the divergent social realities of neighborhoods. They challenge criminologists to appreciate a solidly sociological view of race, crime, and urban inequality.
